turns.content: quick question. how do you deal with feeling like everyone depends on you and you cant say no to any of it
That's a big question, and the fact that you're asking it suggests you're feeling stretched pretty thin. Can you give me some context — is this about work, family, or both?

family mainly. so I'm the eldest of 6 kids right. somali family so big households are normal loool. but being the oldest means everyone comes to you for everything. money, advice, help with homework, dealing with my mum when she's stressed. it's constant
Being the oldest of six is a huge amount of responsibility, especially in a culture where that role carries real weight. How old are your siblings?

24, 22, 19, 16, and 12. so the older ones are semi-independent but the younger two still need bare supervision and my mum cant do it all on her own. I basically helped raise them tbh. like when I was 14 I was doing school runs and cooking dinner for everyone

You've been parenting since you were 14 — that's a lot to carry, even now. The fact that you're still the go-to person even though some of your siblings are adults themselves says a lot about the dynamic. What's triggering this right now? Has something specific come up?

my brother Yusuf — the 16 year old — is being a nightmare at school. getting detentions, not doing his coursework. my mum called me in tears about it yesterday and I'm sat at my desk at work trying to calm her down while also finalising audit workpapers. like I cant be in two places at once

That's an impossible position. Your mum reaches out to you because she trusts you, but you're dealing with your own demanding job at the same time. With Yusuf — do you think it's typical teenage stuff or something deeper going on with him?

honestly I think hes just being 16. his mates are a bit rough and he wants to impress them. but my mum panics because she doesnt want him going down the wrong path. which I get. but then the expectation is that I somehow fix it?? like I'm not his parent I'm his sister

That's exactly it — you're his sister, not his parent, but you've been put in a parental role for so long that the boundary has blurred. It's reasonable for your mum to worry, but it's also reasonable for you to feel overwhelmed. Have you ever talked to your mum about how much this takes out of you?

wallah I cant do that to her. she's been through so much and she works so hard. if I tell her I'm struggling she'll just feel guilty and add that onto everything else she's carrying. so I just deal with it

I understand the instinct to protect her from your own stress. But "just dealing with it" indefinitely isn't sustainable — you've essentially been absorbing everyone's stress on top of your own for years. What about your 24 and 22-year-old siblings? Can any of the load shift to them?

my sister Amina is 24 and she does help sometimes but shes also doing her own thing. and my brother Hassan is 22 but honestly he doesnt step up the way he should. which annoys me because I was doing way more at his age. but I also know thats partly because I made it easy for everyone by just doing everything myself

That's a really self-aware observation. When the oldest takes on everything, it can accidentally train everyone else to not step up. It's not your fault — you did what was needed at the time — but it means the pattern won't break unless you actively change it. Could you start delegating small things to Hassan specifically? Not a big confrontation, just "can you handle this one thing this week."
yeah maybe. like with the Yusuf thing I could ask Hassan to actually go sit with him and have a chat instead of me trying to do it over the phone while I'm at work. Hassan lives closer to mum anyway

That's a perfect example. Hassan is closer, he's 22 — he's old enough to have a real conversation with a 16-year-old about school. And it might actually land differently coming from a brother who's nearer his age. You don't have to do everything yourself to be a good oldest sibling.

I know that logically but emotionally its hard init. like when youre the eldest everyone just expects you to have it all figured out whether you like it or not. and I love my family wallah I'd do anything for them. I just wish I could split myself in half sometimes

The pressure of being the eldest is real — everyone looks to you like you've got it all figured out, when in reality you're still figuring out your own life too. Loving them and needing boundaries aren't opposites. You can be there for your family without being the sole fixer. Start small with Hassan this week — ask him to check in on Yusuf. See how it goes.

yeah I'm gonna text him tonight. you're right I need to stop doing everything solo. anyway thanks for this, sometimes you just need someone to tell you its okay to not be the solution to every problem loool
It's more than okay — it's necessary. You can't pour from an empty cup, as the saying goes. Text Hassan, let him take this one, and see how it feels to step back just slightly. Good luck with it.
